summary: I grew up in South Pasadena, California, where I attended elementary school and graduated from South Pasadena High School. It was a formative place for me during my boyhood years in the early 1900s. The community and my early education there laid the groundwork for my later pursuits in college and printing.

I was born in Los Angeles in 1905 and spent much of my early life in the surrounding areas. Later on, Los Angeles became important to my printing career, especially through connections with people like Jake Zeitlin and Bruce McCallister. I also studied printing at the Los Angeles Trade Technical School during 1928 and 1929, which was crucial to my development.

I started college at Occidental College in 1924 and after some time at Stanford and the University of the South, I returned to Occidental to complete my BA degree in 1928. Occidental was where I first seriously began my higher education journey before shifting my focus toward printing and book arts.
cues: returned after Stanford and University of the South

summary: After my freshman year at Occidental College, I was admitted to Stanford University, where I spent my sophomore year. It was part of my early college experience before I attended the University of the South and then returned to Occidental. Stanford was an important step in my academic path during the mid-1920s.

Following my time at Stanford, I attended the University of the South at Sewanee, Tennessee. Although I didn’t complete my degree there, the experience broadened my perspective before I returned to Occidental College to finish my BA. It was a brief but meaningful part of my educational journey.

entity: University of Southern California Gould School of Law
summary: After graduating from college in 1928, I enrolled at the University of Southern California's School of Law, intending to pursue a legal career. However, within a year, I realized law was not the right path for me. During this time, I discovered the writings of Thomas James Cobden-Sanderson, which inspired me to shift toward the graphic arts.

I came across The Journals of Thomas James Cobden-Sanderson while exploring bookstores during my time at law school. His philosophy of independent creativity deeply inspired me and played a pivotal role in my decision to leave law and pursue a career in printing and book arts. His work remains a significant influence on my approach to craftsmanship.

When I sought advice on printing, I was told that John Henry Nash was one of the best printers on the West Coast. Although I never apprenticed directly under him, his reputation and work were influential in shaping my understanding of fine printing. He was part of the community of printers I admired during my early career.

The Grabhorn Brothers of the Grabhorn Press in San Francisco were also recognized as some of the finest printers on the West Coast. Their work, alongside John Henry Nash’s, helped guide my early printing ambitions. I looked up to their craftsmanship while developing my own skills in Los Angeles.

Jake Zeitlin was a key figure in Los Angeles who encouraged me in my printing pursuits. Along with Bruce McCallister, he supported my studies and early work in printing during the late 1920s. Their encouragement was invaluable as I developed my craft at the Los Angeles Trade Technical School.

Bruce McCallister, like Jake Zeitlin, was an important mentor and supporter in Los Angeles during my early printing career. Their guidance helped me navigate the world of fine printing and book production as I studied and practiced in the late 1920s. Their influence was a cornerstone of my development.

I studied printing at the Los Angeles Trade Technical School during 1928 and 1929, where I had access to excellent equipment and instruction. My tutor, James Hallack, was very supportive and helped me refine my skills. This formal training was crucial in launching my career in fine printing.

James Hallack was my tutor at the Los Angeles Trade Technical School and played a vital role in my printing education. His support and guidance during 1928 and 1929 helped me gain confidence and technical skills. I am grateful for his mentorship during those formative years.

I rented a studio in Clyde Browne's Abbey San Encino located in Highland Park, Los Angeles, where I set up my own press. This space allowed me to print small booklets and pamphlets of poetry by notable poets. Clyde Browne's Abbey San Encino was an important creative hub for me in the late 1920s.

Abbey San Encino in Highland Park was where I established my own press in a rented studio during the late 1920s. It was a lively place that supported my early printing projects, including small booklets of poetry. The Abbey provided a creative environment that was essential to my growth as a printer.

Highland Park, Los Angeles, was the neighborhood where Abbey San Encino was located, and where I set up my printing press. This area became a significant part of my early career as I printed poetry pamphlets and honed my craft. It was a vibrant community that fostered artistic endeavors.

I printed small booklets of poems by Carl Sandburg among other poets during my early years in printing. These pamphlets were quick projects but deeply satisfying, allowing me to engage with contemporary poetry and fine printing. Sandburg's work was part of the literary circle I admired and promoted.

Archibald MacLeish was one of the poets whose work I printed in small booklets during my early printing career. These projects helped me develop my skills and connect with the literary culture of the time. MacLeish’s poetry was part of the rich material I enjoyed producing.

Leonie Adams was among the poets whose pamphlets I printed in my rented studio at Abbey San Encino. Printing her poetry was part of my early exploration of fine printing and book arts. These small projects were both enjoyable and educational for me.

Robinson Jeffers was another poet whose work I printed in small booklets during my early printing days. These pamphlets allowed me to practice my craft and engage with contemporary poetry. Jeffers’ poetry was part of the literary community I sought to support through my printing.

I worked at Vroman's Bookstore in Pasadena until late spring of 1930. This job gave me the opportunity to immerse myself in books and printing culture while I pursued my own printing projects in my spare time. Vroman's was an important part of my life during this period.

I became deeply interested in Francois-Louis Schmied, a Parisian printer and book designer, after reading about him while working at Vroman's. In 1930, I traveled to Europe and stayed in Paris to apprentice with Schmied at his atelier. This apprenticeship was a rich experience that greatly influenced my later work and the founding of the Ward Ritchie Press.

In early June 1930, I traveled to Europe with my mother and aunt, and when we reached Paris, I decided to stay to apprentice with Francois-Louis Schmied. Paris was the center of my artistic development during this time, where I learned many skills that shaped my printing philosophy. The city was a pivotal place in my career.

After returning from Paris, I established the Ward Ritchie Press in 1932. The skills and philosophy I absorbed during my apprenticeship with Francois-Louis Schmied strongly influenced the press's approach to fine printing and book production. This press became the foundation of my professional life.

My aunt lived in Michigan and was planning a European tour in 1930, inviting my mother to accompany her. My mother persuaded me to join them, which led to my trip to Europe and ultimately my apprenticeship in Paris. Michigan was indirectly important as the starting point for this pivotal journey.
